claim: For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if (n-1)! + 1 is divisible by n.
If n is prime, then (n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n).
If n is composite and n > 4, then (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For any prime p, (p-1)! + 1 is divisible by p but not by p².
For composite n > 4, the largest prime divisor of n divides (n-1)!.
For prime p > 2, (p-1)! is odd.
For odd prime p, (p-1)! + 1 is even.
For prime p ≥ 5, (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p) and (p-1)! ≡ 0 (mod 2).
n is composite (n > 4) if and only if (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n), while n is prime iff (n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n).
For prime p, the sum of all residues 1, 2, ..., p-1 modulo p equals p(p-1)/2 ≡ 0 (mod p) for odd p.
For prime p, -1 has a square root modulo p if and only if p ≡ 1 (mod 4) or p = 2.
For prime p, 1 · 2 · ... · (p-1) ≡ -1 (mod p) implies ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} k^{-1} ≡ 0 (mod p).
For composite n = p·q where p, q are distinct odd primes, (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For composite n = 4, (3)! = 6 ≡ 2 (mod 4), so 4 is the unique composite where factorial(n-1) is not divisible by n.
For composite n ≠ 4, (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For prime p > 2, ⌊(p-1)!/p⌋ is odd.
The set of integers n where (n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n) is exactly the set of primes.
For prime p, if k divides (p-1)!, then gcd(k, p) = 1.
For prime p ≡ 1 (mod 4), (p-1)! is a quadratic residue modulo p.
For prime p ≡ 3 (mod 4), (p-1)! is a quadratic non-residue modulo p.
For prime p, the product of all primitive roots modulo p equals (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p).
The set of composite integers n > 4 equals the set of integers where (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For prime p > 2, φ(p) = p - 1 and (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p).
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or ((phi(n) == n - 1) and ((factorial(n-1) % n) == (n-1)))

claim: For prime p, (φ(p) - 1)! ≡ -1 (mod p).
For prime p, τ(p) = 2 (number of divisors).
For prime p, σ(p) = p + 1 (sum of divisors).
For prime p, (p-1)! + 1 is divisible by σ(p) - 1 = p.
For composite n = p² where p is an odd prime and p² > p, (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod p²).
predicate: (is_prime(n)) or (not ((any((n % (pow(p, 2)) == 0) for p in range(2, int(pow(n, 0.5)) + 1))))) or ((factorial(n-1) % n) == 0)

claim: For prime p ≡ 1 (mod 4), the number of solutions to x² ≡ -1 (mod p) is exactly 2.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or ((n % 4) != 1) or ((pow(1, 2, n) == n - 1) or (pow(n - 1, 2, n) == n - 1))

claim: For prime p > 2, the group (ℤ/pℤ)* has order φ(p) and (p-1)! generates a non-identity element.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or ((phi(n) == n - 1) and ((factorial(n-1) % n) != 1))

claim: For composite n > 4, the product of all integers 1..n-1 is divisible by n.
For prime p > 2, gcd((p-1)! + 1, p) = p.
For n > 4 composite, gcd((n-1)!, n) = n.
For prime p, -1 is a p-th power residue modulo p.
For odd prime p, the sum 1 + 2 + ... + (p-1) ≡ 0 (mod p).
For prime p, the product (1 · 2 · ... · (p-1))² ≡ (-1)^{p-1} (mod p).
For prime p ≡ 1 (mod 4), (p-1)!² ≡ 1 (mod p).
For prime p ≡ 3 (mod 4), (p-1)!² ≡ -1 (mod p) is impossible.
For odd prime p, -1 is a quadratic residue mod p iff p ≡ 1 (mod 4).
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or (((n % 4) == 1) == (any((pow(x, 2, n)) == (n-1) for x in range(n))))

claim: For composite n > 4, (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n) and gcd((n-1)!, n) = n.
predicate: (is_prime(n)) or (n <= 4) or (((factorial(n-1) % n) == 0) and (gcd(factorial(n-1), n) == n))

claim: For prime p, vp((p-1)!, p) = 0 (p does not divide (p-1)!).
For composite n > 4 with smallest prime divisor p, p divides (n-1)!.
For prime p, (p-1)! and p are coprime.
For odd prime p, (1 + 2 + ... + (p-1))² ≡ 0 (mod p).
For prime p, (p-1)! generates the cyclic multiplicative group (ℤ/pℤ)* if p is a Gauss prime.
The primality of n is uniquely determined by the congruence class of (n-1)! modulo n: prime iff ≡ -1 (mod n), composite (n > 4) iff ≡ 0 (mod n).
predicate: ((is_prime(n)) == ((factorial(n-1) % n) == (n-1))) or (not is_prime(n) and n > 4 and ((factorial(n-1) % n) == 0))

claim: For prime p ≡ 1 (mod 4), (p-1)! ≡ ±√{-1} (mod p) where √{-1} exists.
For prime p, (p-1)! + 1 is divisible by p exactly once; higher prime powers of p do not divide (p-1)! + 1 in general.
For prime p > 2, (φ(p))! ≡ -1 (mod p) and φ(φ(p)) = φ(p-1) divides the order of this element.
